PC - Crown Guard

factions/pc---crown-guard.content:
At A Glance
The Crown Guard serves as the primary law enforcement and stabilising force throughout Velmoran territory. Charged with maintaining order, protecting trade routes and responding to threats beyond the capacity of local authorities, their presence can be found across much of Aedryn.
While often viewed as a military organisation, the Crown Guard functions as both a policing force and a defensive institution. Their role is not to eliminate every danger, but to prevent instability from spreading and threatening the wider region.
The organisation is divided into several regional divisions, each adapted to the unique challenges of the territories they oversee. From the trade hubs of the the Shattercoast to the borders of the The Stoneward Spine, the Crown Guard acts as the visible extension of Velmoran authority.
Among ordinary citizens, the Crown Guard inspires a mixture of trust, respect and caution.
Related Articles


PC - The Velmor


PC - Velmora


PC - The Shattercoast


PC - The Stoneward Spine


PC - The Verdant Expanse

factions/pc---the-ellei.content:
At A Glance
The Ellei are the dominant power within the Verdant Expanse and are composed primarily of elves, monkey folk and the many smaller societies that call the great forests home.
Of all the major factions in Aedryn, the Ellei are considered the most unified. They share a common belief in preserving the natural world and maintaining a balance between civilisation and the forces of nature.
The Ellei possess some of the continent’s most accomplished mages and are renowned for their understanding of the Rift and its influence upon the world. While they are often viewed as isolationist by outsiders, the Ellei see themselves not as withdrawn, but as guardians of something worth protecting.
Their military is widely regarded as the best trained in Aedryn, though they favour defence and preservation over conquest and expansion.
Related Articles


PC - The Verdant Expanse


PC - The Rift


PC - The Smoke War

factions/pc---the-folkmoor-creed.content:
At A Glance
The Folkmoor Creed is the dominant power of the Shattercoast and the faction responsible for overseeing much of the trade that flows throughout Aedryn and beyond. Born from cooperation between displaced Orcs and Wyrwood following the fall of the Dwarven Empire, the Creed built its influence not through conquest, but through commerce.
The people of the Creed value independence, initiative and practicality. Wealth, reputation and results often carry more weight than noble titles or political ideals. Their ports connect Aedryn to distant Shards, and few goods move across the continent without passing through their hands at some point.
The Creed maintains cordial relations with the other major factions, acting as the bridge between them through trade and diplomacy. While they rarely project military power openly, their influence is felt wherever merchants gather and deals are struck.
Many see the Creed as the engine that keeps the continent moving.
Others quietly wonder how much passes through their ports without anyone noticing.
Related Articles


PC - The Shattercoast


PC - The Smoke War

factions/pc---the-redentan.content:
At A Glance
The Redentan are the dominant faction of the Stoneward Spine occupying the former Dwarven Holds following the collapse of the Dwarven Empire during the Smoke War.
Known for their curiosity, ingenuity and relentless pursuit of knowledge, the Redentan have become the foremost technological power in Aedryn. Their engineers continue to recover, study and improve upon the remnants of dwarven innovation, creating technologies unlike anything found elsewhere on the continent.
Although they maintain the smallest standing military among the major factions, their defensive capabilities are widely respected. The Redentan have little interest in conquest. Their focus is ensuring that they are never again forced to live under the rule of others.
Many outsiders view the Redentan as inventors and scholars. The Redentan themselves would likely prefer a simpler description.
Prepared.
Related Articles


PC - The Stoneward Spine


PC - The Smoke War

factions/pc---the-velmor.content:
At A Glance
The Velmor are the dominant political power of modern Aedryn and the driving force behind much of the continent’s reconstruction following the Smoke War. Their influence extends far beyond their borders through trade, diplomacy and the actions of the PC - Crown Guard.
Velmoran culture values ambition, productivity and civic responsibility. Many believe prosperity is built through hard work, strong institutions and a willingness to look towards the future rather than dwell upon the past.
As the largest and most influential faction on the continent, the Velmor often find themselves at the centre of Aedryn’s political affairs. To their supporters they are the architects of stability. To their critics they are a nation increasingly convinced that it knows what is best for everyone else.
Related Articles


PC - Velmora


PC - Crown Guard


PC - The Smoke War

locations/aedryn/pc---aedryn.content:
At A Glance
Aedryn is one of the two major continents of Thyren and the primary setting of the Nerra campaign. Once dominated by the Dwarven Empire, the continent is now divided amongst several major powers, each shaped by the legacy of the Smoke War and the challenges of rebuilding a fractured world.
The continent is home to four major regions: the industrious Stoneward Spine, the wild Verdant Expanse, the trade-focused Shattercoast, and the prosperous heartlands of Velmora. Though united by geography, each region possesses its own cultures, beliefs and ambitions.
Despite decades of relative peace, the scars of history remain visible throughout Aedryn. Ancient ruins dot the landscape, tensions linger between old allies and rivals, and the consequences of the Cataclysm can still be felt across the continent.
Overview
To most of its inhabitants, Aedryn is the centre of the world.
Its roads connect great cities and isolated villages alike. Merchant caravans cross its plains. Airships rise from its ports. Armies march across its borders. Generations have lived and died without ever seeing the lands that lie beyond its shores.
Modern Aedryn was forged in the aftermath of the Smoke War. The fall of the Dwarven Empire reshaped the political landscape, giving rise to the major factions that govern the continent today. While the war brought an end to imperial rule, it did not erase the divisions, memories and competing interests that continue to influence modern life.
The continent stretches from the dense forests and jungles of the Verdant Expanse to the towering peaks of the Stoneward Spine. Along its northern coast, the Shattercoast serves as a gateway to the wider world, while Velmora’s cities drive much of the continent’s trade, politics and development.
Most people view Aedryn as a land of opportunity. Others see it as a continent still learning how to live with the consequences of its past. ,Both are probably correct.
Related Articles


PC - Velmora


PC - The Verdant Expanse


PC - The Shattercoast


PC - The Stoneward Spine


PC - The Smoke War


PC - The Cataclysm

locations/aedryn/shattercoast/pc---the-s.content:
The Shattercoast is where the edge of civilisation meets the edge of the world.
Stretching along Aedryn’s northern coastline, the region is famous for its trade, shipping, salvage operations and wealth. Airships and merchant vessels constantly move through its ports, carrying goods from across Nerra.
Life on the Shattercoast is rarely predictable. The region is known for its independent spirit, unusual customs and willingness to embrace opportunities that other parts of Aedryn might consider reckless. Coin speaks loudly here, and practicality often matters more than tradition.
The people of the coast are heavily influenced by the Folkmoor Creed, a powerful alliance of merchants, traders, sailors and financiers that emerged following the Smoke War. Their influence extends far beyond the coastline itself.
The Shattercoast also sits closer to the Sunders than most people would prefer. Strange weather, unusual phenomena and unsettling sailor tales are all common parts of life. Most are likely exaggerations.
Related Articles:
PC - The Folkmoor Creed

locations/aedryn/stoneward-spine/pc---th.content:
The Stoneward Spine dominates the western horizon of Aedryn.
A vast mountain range of towering peaks, deep valleys and sprawling underground holds, it was once the heartland of the dwarven empire that ruled much of the continent during the Smoke War. Even today, the mountains remain shaped by that legacy.
Much of the Spine is now inhabited by the Redentan, whose settlements and industries have emerged amongst the remnants of dwarven infrastructure. Trade, engineering and technological innovation play a significant role throughout the region, with many of Aedryn’s most advanced industries tracing their origins back to the mountains.
Despite ongoing rebuilding efforts, the Spine remains a place of mixed emotions throughout the continent. Some see it as a symbol of liberation and progress. Others see it as a reminder of occupation, war and old wounds that have never fully healed.
Travellers passing through the region will encounter bustling industrial settlements, ancient stone roads, abandoned fortifications and countless reminders of the conflict that shaped modern Aedryn.
Related Articles
PC - The Redentan
PC - The Smoke War

locations/aedryn/velmora/needleberry/pc-.content:
At A Glance
Needleberry is a small rural town located in southern Aedryn, near the foothills of the Stoneward Spine. Known for its muddy roads, ageing buildings and hardworking residents, it serves as a waypoint for travellers moving between the frontier settlements and the wider world.
Though far from the centres of political power, Needleberry remains an important stop for merchants, labourers and adventurers travelling through the region.
Overview
Few people set out to visit Needleberry. Fewer still stay longer than they need to.
Nestled amongst rolling hills and muddy roads on the fringes of civilisation, Needleberry is the sort of place travellers pass through on their way somewhere more important. The town is small, weathered and unremarkable by most maps. Its timber buildings lean slightly with age, its roads become thick with mud after even the lightest rain, and many of its fences seem to have been repaired so many times that nobody remembers what the original timber looked like.
Needleberry has a reputation amongst travellers as a place where caution and kindness exist side by side. Most people are willing to lend a hand if someone is in need, though they would probably recommend keeping one hand near their coin purse while doing so. The town sits far enough from major population centres that law and order can occasionally become flexible, particularly when the nearest authority is days away.
At the heart of the town sits The Bent Needle, a well-loved tavern that serves as Needleberry’s unofficial gathering place. If there is news to be heard, work to be found, or trouble brewing, it will eventually find its way through the tavern doors.
To most of Aedryn, Needleberry is forgettable. To the people who call it home, it is simply home.
Related Articles


PC - The Stoneward Spine


PC - Velmora


PC - The Bent Needle
The Tavern in the Town of Needleberry (PC - Needleberry)

locations/aedryn/velmora/pc---velmora.content:
If Aedryn has a heart, it beats within Velmora.
Stretching across much of central Aedryn, Velmora is the largest political power on the continent and the region most heavily associated with rebuilding following the Smoke War. Its roads are maintained, its cities are growing, and its influence can be felt almost everywhere.  The commanding power of the region are the Velmor.
The people of Velmora value hard work, ambition and perseverance. Many believe that civilisation survives because ordinary people continue getting up every morning and doing what needs to be done. This mentality helped shape much of the modern continent after the war.
Yet beneath the prosperity lies tension. The war may have ended, but few have forgotten it. Veterans are common throughout the region, political disagreements simmer beneath the surface, and the presence of the Crown Guard serves as a constant reminder that peace is not something to be taken for granted.
Related Articles
PC - The Velmor
PC - The Smoke War

locations/aedryn/verdant-expanse/pc---th.content:
The Verdant Expanse is one of the oldest and least understood regions of Aedryn.
Vast forests, dense jungles and ancient wilderness stretch across much of the region, swallowing roads, ruins and entire settlements beneath layers of growth. Many places within the Expanse have remained untouched for generations, while others are known only through stories and half-forgotten maps.
The region is home to many elves, vanara and lizardfolk, though numerous smaller communities exist hidden amongst the wilderness. Outsiders are often treated cautiously, particularly in the deeper reaches of the Expanse, where trust is earned slowly and traditions run deep.
The people of the Expanse tend to view the outside world with a degree of scepticism. They have watched empires rise and fall, wars come and go, and many believe civilisation has a habit of creating its own problems.
Travellers often speak of strange ruins hidden beneath the trees, forgotten roads reclaimed by nature, and creatures older than recorded history moving through the deepest wilderness.
Whether such stories are true is another matter entirely.
Related Articles
PC - The Ellei
PC - The Smoke War

lore/history/pc---shards.content:
At A Glance
The Shards are the great landmasses of Nerra and serve as the closest equivalent to continents in the modern world. Each Shard is separated from the others by the vast voids known as the Sunders, making travel between them difficult, expensive and often dangerous.
Most scholars believe the Shards were formed during the aftermath of the Cataclysm, when the world fractured into the divided form known today. Whether the Shards were once connected as a single landmass remains one of the most widely accepted theories regarding the ancient world.
Five major Shards dominate modern maps: Thyren, Kareth, Caelyx, Morvane and Brassreach. Each possesses its own cultures, political powers, histories and dangers. While trade and communication exist between them, most people will spend their entire lives without ever setting foot upon another Shard.
To many, the Shards represent more than geography. They are a reminder that the world was once something greater, and perhaps something far stranger, than it appears today.
Historical Account
Excerpt from The Divided World: A Survey of the Great Shards of Nerra, authored by Cartographer-Magister Elric Thorne of the Royal Geographic Society of Velor.

To understand modern Nerra, one must first understand the Shards.
Most travellers spend their lives concerned with kingdoms, cities and borders. These things are certainly important, but they are ultimately temporary. Governments rise and fall. Empires expand and collapse. Roads appear and disappear beneath the passage of centuries.
The Shards endure.
No matter where one travels within the known world, the presence of the Shards shapes every aspect of life. Trade routes, political alliances, migration, religion and warfare all exist within the constraints imposed by these colossal divisions of land.
What exactly a Shard is remains a surprisingly difficult question to answer.
The common description is that they are continents separated by the Sunders. While broadly accurate, this explanation ignores the unusual nature of their formation. Viewed from above, many Shards resemble immense craters or basins, surrounded by towering stone walls that rise above the lands contained within them. Some scholars argue this is evidence of catastrophic geological upheaval. Others suggest the very laws governing the world were altered during the Cataclysm.
As with so many subjects relating to that ancient disaster, certainty remains elusive.
Of the known Shards, Thyren is the largest and most influential. It is home to the continents of Aedryn and Evellon and serves as the political and economic heart of much of modern civilisation. Most major powers maintain some presence within Thyren, and many who have never travelled beyond its borders mistakenly assume it represents the entirety of the world.
Kareth occupies a position near the centre of the known world and has earned a reputation as a hub of commerce and trade. Goods from every corner of Nerra eventually find their way through Kareth’s markets, making it one of the wealthiest regions known to modern scholars.
Caelyx remains a source of fascination and concern. The Shard’s long association with the Rift has inspired countless theories regarding its history. Some claim the Cataclysm struck Caelyx harder than anywhere else. Others insist the Shard played a role in the disaster itself. Reliable evidence supporting either claim remains frustratingly scarce.
Morvane is perhaps the least understood of the major Shards. What little information reaches the wider world often comes from sailors, explorers and merchants whose stories rarely agree. Most descriptions depict a land still scarred by ancient devastation, though separating truth from embellishment has proven difficult.
Brassreach stands apart from the others. Renowned for its industry and technological sophistication, the Shard has developed a reputation for innovation that rivals even the greatest achievements of the former Dwarven Empire. Visitors often return with stories of machinery and inventions that seem decades ahead of anything found elsewhere in Nerra.
Despite their differences, the Shards share one common truth.
They are separated.
The Sunders ensure that no people, kingdom or empire can ever truly dominate the entire world. Every voyage between Shards remains a deliberate undertaking. Every trade route exists at significant risk. Every traveller who crosses the void is reminded that the world itself bears scars older than any living civilisation.
Perhaps that is why the Shards continue to capture the imagination.
They are not merely places.
They are fragments.
Remnants of a world that once existed and a constant reminder that no one alive truly knows what was lost when it broke.

Archivist’s Note: Magister Thorne spent nearly three decades compiling records from merchants, explorers, naval captains and scholarly institutions throughout Nerra. While generally considered reliable, critics argue that his work occasionally favours compelling narratives over hard evidence, particularly regarding the origins of the Shards.

lore/history/pc---sunders.content:
At A Glance
The Sunders are vast voids that separate the major landmasses of Nerra, known as Shards. Most scholars believe they were created during the aftermath of the Cataclysm, though exactly how and why remains the subject of ongoing debate.
Travel between Shards is possible but dangerous. Most journeys are undertaken aboard specialised airships capable of crossing the immense distances between continents. The Sunders are notorious for violent weather, magical disturbances, and creatures that few are willing to describe in detail.
For most people, the Sunders are little more than a distant feature of the world. For sailors, merchants, explorers and adventurers, they are a constant reminder that Nerra was once something very different from the world that exists today.
Historical Account
Excerpt from Crossing the Edge: A Survey of Modern Shard Travel, compiled by Cartographer-General Ellias Thorn of the Royal Geographic Society of Velor.

There is perhaps no sight in all of Nerra more unsettling than one’s first view of a Sunder.
Descriptions rarely prepare the traveller adequately. Most imagine a canyon, a great valley, or some impossibly large ravine. Such comparisons inevitably fall short. A Sunder is not merely a wound in the earth. It is the absence of earth entirely.
The world simply ends.
Across Nerra, the great continental landmasses known as Shards stand separated by vast expanses of empty space. Between them lie the Sunders, colossal voids stretching beyond the horizon, their depths vanishing into darkness so profound that even experienced scholars struggle to explain what lies below.
Most historians agree that the Sunders emerged during the aftermath of the Cataclysm. Prior to that event, ancient records suggest the world existed as a far more unified landmass. Whether the Sunders were created by the Cataclysm itself or merely revealed by it remains a subject of considerable debate.
For ordinary people, such questions are largely academic.
The Sunders are dangerous regardless of their origin.
Travel between Shards remains possible, though never entirely safe. Airships serve as the primary means of crossing these vast divides, their crews often spending years learning the unique hazards associated with Sunder travel. Strong winds, sudden magical disturbances, inexplicable storms and hostile creatures are all common reports from those who make their living navigating the void.
Many captains possess stories they refuse to tell while sober.
Others refuse to speak of certain voyages at all.
It is not uncommon for sailors to describe strange lights drifting beneath their vessels. Some report hearing voices carried on winds where no wind should exist. Entire crews have vanished during crossings under circumstances that remain unexplained. While scholars dismiss many such tales as superstition, the number of similar accounts collected over the centuries has led some to question whether all of them can be ignored.
The creatures that inhabit the Sunders are equally mysterious.
Merchants speak of immense winged predators capable of tearing apart smaller vessels. Explorers tell stories of things moving through the darkness far below, shapes so large that estimating their size becomes impossible. Most such reports remain unverified, though airship captains are generally unwilling to dismiss them outright.
As a result, nearly every nation maintains laws governing Sunder travel.
Some restrict access entirely. Others require special licences, military escorts or approved trade routes. Even in the modern era, crossing between Shards remains a journey most people will never undertake.
For many citizens of Aedryn, the neighbouring Shards might as well be separate worlds.
Perhaps that is why the Sunders inspire such fascination.
They serve as a constant reminder that the world was once something different. Something larger. Something whole.
What was lost during the Cataclysm remains uncertain.
The Sunders ensure that nobody is allowed to forget that something was.

Archivist’s Note: While Cartographer-General Thorn’s work is widely regarded as one of the most reliable modern sources concerning inter-Shard travel, critics note that many sections rely heavily upon testimony from merchants, sailors and explorers. As with many subjects relating to the Sunders, separating fact from folklore remains a difficult task.

lore/history/pc---the-cataclysm.content:
At A Glance
The Cataclysm was the single most devastating event in recorded history and marks the dividing line between the ancient world and the modern age. Although its exact cause remains unknown, nearly every scholar, faith and civilisation agrees that the world was fundamentally changed by it.
The event is believed to have triggered widespread planar instability, reshaped much of Nerra, and coincided with the appearance of the Rift. Entire nations vanished, coastlines shifted, and countless historical records were lost. Many of the world’s greatest mysteries can be traced back to this period.
The Cataclysm also preceded the formation of the Sunders, the vast voids that now separate the major landmasses of Nerra. Whether these fractures were a direct result of the disaster or merely one of its many consequences remains fiercely debated.
Despite centuries of study, no universally accepted explanation for the Cataclysm exists. To some, it was a divine tragedy. To others, an arcane disaster. Many believe the truth was lost alongside the world that existed before it.
Historical Account
Excerpt from Fragments of a Broken World: A Study of the Cataclysm, authored by Scholar-Archivist Theodric Vane of the Astral Collegium.

There is a question that has haunted every age since the world was broken.
What happened?
Entire libraries have been filled attempting to answer it. Kings have funded expeditions in search of forgotten truths. Priests have devoted lifetimes to prayer and contemplation. Scholars have argued, fought and occasionally killed one another in pursuit of answers.
Yet after centuries of investigation, the Cataclysm remains as elusive as ever.
What little can be stated with confidence is frightening enough.
Before the Cataclysm, the world appears to have been very different. Ancient maps depict lands no longer found on any modern chart. References survive to cities, kingdoms and peoples that seemingly vanished overnight. In some cases, entire cultures exist only as names preserved in damaged records.
Then, at some point in the distant past, everything changed.
The planes became unstable. Natural laws appear to have faltered. Vast regions were devastated or altered beyond recognition. Survivors recorded impossible storms, ruptures in reality, and phenomena that modern scholars struggle to explain. In many surviving accounts, there is a recurring sense of confusion. Those who lived through the disaster often seemed incapable of understanding what was unfolding around them.
Perhaps the most enduring legacy of the Cataclysm is the appearance of the Rift.
Whether the Rift caused the disaster, emerged because of it, or merely became visible during the chaos remains unknown. What is known is that references to the Rift become dramatically more common in historical records following the Cataclysm. Since then, it has remained one of the greatest sources of fear, fascination and scholarly inquiry within Nerra.
The Sunders also appear shortly after this period. Where once there may have been continuous land, now there are only impossible voids separating the world’s great Shards. The exact process by which these fractures formed remains one of the most contested subjects in modern academia.
Religion offers no shortage of explanations.
Some faiths teach that the gods waged war against one another. Others claim the mortal world was punished for some forgotten transgression. Certain sects insist the Cataclysm was a necessary sacrifice that prevented an even greater disaster. Unsurprisingly, these claims rarely agree with one another.
The difficulty is that nearly every explanation suffers from the same flaw.
Evidence.
The Cataclysm destroyed so much that any investigation into its causes inevitably becomes an investigation into absence. Historians do not study what survived. They study what is missing.
Missing records.
Missing civilisations.
Missing truths.
Even today, expeditions occasionally uncover ruins that appear older than the Cataclysm itself. Such discoveries often create more questions than answers. Every fragment recovered seems only to reveal how much has been lost.
Perhaps that is why the Cataclysm continues to fascinate scholars.
Most historical mysteries concern events that happened.
The Cataclysm concerns a world that no longer exists.
And until the truth is discovered, every generation will continue asking the same question as the last.
What happened?

Archivist’s Note: Scholar-Archivist Vane’s work is considered one of the most comprehensive modern studies of the Cataclysm. Critics have noted that his conclusions often favour uncertainty where stronger theories may exist. Vane himself famously responded to such criticism by writing, “Confidence and evidence are not the same thing.”

lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---other-sm.filePath: Lore/History/The Smoke War/PC - Other Smoke War Events.md
lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---other-sm.content:
Other Notable Events
While the Night of Red Smoke is often viewed as the spark that ignited the Smoke War, several other events proved equally important in the eventual collapse of the Dwarven Empire.
The Ratfolk Betrayal
For generations, Ratfolk lived within the shadows of the Dwarven Holds. Though largely ignored by the Empire, they possessed an intimate understanding of its tunnels, infrastructure and supply routes. When the Ratfolk openly pledged themselves to the Velmoran Alliance, they provided information that allowed Alliance forces to penetrate strongholds previously considered impregnable. Many historians consider their involvement one of the decisive factors in the fall of the Stoneward Spine.
The Automaton Uprisings
Originally created by the dwarves as labourers and soldiers, automatons served throughout the Empire for generations. During the war, increasing numbers began turning against their creators and joining the Velmoran Alliance. Their knowledge of Dwarven logistics, production facilities and military operations proved invaluable, while their rebellion became a symbol of liberation across the continent.
The Orc Rebellions
Unlike the automatons, the orcs required no liberation before rising against the Empire. Having endured generations of subjugation following their defeat in the mountains, many tribes launched their own coordinated uprisings during the war. These rebellions would eventually align with the Velmoran Alliance, creating a common front against Dwarven rule and stretching imperial resources across multiple fronts.
The Political Fractures
Many historians believe the Empire’s enemies were not solely responsible for its collapse. Persistent rumours speak of political rivalries, betrayals and internal divisions within the Dwarven leadership during the final years of the war. While evidence remains scarce, few scholars dispute that the Empire was struggling to maintain unity long before its final defeat.
The Fall of the Empire
Ultimately, the combined pressure of rebellion, invasion, overextended supply lines and internal instability proved too much to withstand. The Dwarven Empire collapsed, its territories divided amongst the victors and its people driven from much of the Stoneward Spine.

lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-dwar.filePath: Lore/History/The Smoke War/PC - The Dwarven Empire.md
lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-dwar.content:
At A Glance
The Dwarven Empire was once the dominant power of Aedryn, controlling vast territories from its strongholds within the Stoneward Spine. Renowned for its engineering, industry and military strength, the Empire shaped much of the modern world long before the Smoke War.
At its height, the Empire connected distant regions through trade, infrastructure and technological innovation. Its influence could be felt across the entire continent.
The Empire eventually collapsed during the Smoke War following years of rebellion, political instability and military defeat. Though the Empire is gone, its legacy remains visible throughout Aedryn in the form of ancient Holds, forgotten technologies and the societies that rose in its wake.

Historical Account
Excerpt from Reflections on Empire: A Study of Dwarven Ascendancy by Historian Corvin Hale, Astral Collegium Press.

History has a habit of remembering the end of things more vividly than their beginning.
Mention the Dwarven Empire to most citizens of Aedryn and they will think first of occupation, rebellion and war. They will think of the Smoke War. They will think of the Empire’s fall.
Few stop to ask how it rose.
Long before the modern nations of Aedryn took shape, the dwarves carved vast cities into the Stoneward Spine. Where others saw inhospitable mountains, they saw opportunity. Generations of engineers, miners and inventors transformed stone into industry and industry into prosperity.
The Holds became marvels of the age.
Roads stretched further than any that came before them. Trade flourished. New technologies reshaped daily life. Entire regions benefitted from inventions originating deep beneath the mountains.
Success brought confidence.
Confidence became certainty.
And certainty, as history repeatedly demonstrates, can be a dangerous thing.
As the Empire grew, so too did its belief in its own superiority. Many within its leadership came to view dwarven civilisation as the natural future of Aedryn. Other cultures were seen not as equals, but as peoples who would eventually benefit from dwarven guidance, whether they desired it or not.
Expansion followed.
Then conquest.
Territories were annexed. Populations were brought beneath imperial authority. Resistance was met with increasing force. While many regions prospered materially under imperial rule, they did so at the cost of self-determination.
The contradiction sat at the heart of the Empire.
It built.
It innovated.
It unified.
Yet it demanded obedience in return.
For a time, this bargain held.
Then the cracks began to show.
Rebellions became more frequent. Occupied peoples grew restless. Supply routes stretched across a continent increasingly difficult to control. The Night of Red Smoke transformed resentment into resistance, and resistance into open war.
The Smoke War would ultimately destroy the Empire.
What generations had spent centuries building collapsed within a few decades. The Holds fell. Territories declared independence. Former subjects became new powers.
Yet even now, the Empire’s shadow lingers.
Its roads are still travelled.
Its machines are still studied.
Its cities are still inhabited.
Many of the nations that define modern Aedryn were shaped by resisting the Empire.
Others were shaped by inheriting it.
Whether remembered as tyrants, visionaries or something in between, the dwarves achieved one thing that history rarely grants any civilisation.
They changed the world.
And the world has never entirely moved on.
Related Articles


PC - The Smoke War


PC - The Night of Red Smoke


PC - The Velmoran Alliance


PC - The Stoneward Spine


PC - The Redentan

lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-nigh.filePath: Lore/History/The Smoke War/PC - The Night of Red Smoke.md
lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-nigh.content:
At A Glance
The Night of Red Smoke was a pivotal event during the Dwarven occupation of Aedryn and is widely regarded as one of the turning points that led to the Smoke War.
The incident took place in the city of Karnhold, where a protest against Dwarven rule ended in widespread bloodshed. Exactly what occurred remains heavily debated. What is known is that Dwarven forces responded with overwhelming force, resulting in the deaths of countless civilians and resistance members.
Though the uprising itself failed, many historians regard the Night of Red Smoke as the spark that transformed scattered resentment into organised rebellion across the continent.
Historical Account
Excerpt from The Fall of Empire: A Study of the Smoke War, authored by Professor Aldren Voss of the Astral Collegium.

No event from the years preceding the Smoke War has been studied, debated or politicised more heavily than the Night of Red Smoke.
Even now, decades later, agreement remains elusive.
What can be stated with certainty is remarkably little.
The city of Karnhold had endured Dwarven occupation for many years. Tensions within the city had steadily worsened as resistance movements gained support amongst the local population. By the time of the incident, public frustration with imperial rule had become impossible to ignore.
On the night in question, a large gathering formed within the city.
Why they gathered depends largely upon which source one consults.
Velmoran accounts describe a peaceful demonstration intended to protest occupation and inspire wider resistance. Surviving Dwarven records speak of organised agitators preparing for insurrection. Several independent accounts suggest the truth may have lain somewhere between the two.
What happened next remains the subject of fierce disagreement.
At some point, violence erupted.
Whether this violence originated from protestors, resistance fighters, Dwarven soldiers or some combination thereof has never been conclusively established. Witness testimonies frequently contradict one another, and many records from the period were destroyed during the war.
The response, however, is beyond dispute.
Dwarven forces moved to suppress the gathering.
The operation escalated rapidly.
By dawn, large sections of Karnhold had been reduced to ruins, smoke hung over the city, and the streets were littered with the dead.
No reliable casualty figures exist. Some estimates number the dead in the hundreds. Others place the toll far higher.
In the immediate aftermath, the rebellion had failed. Its leaders were dead, imprisoned or scattered. Order had been restored. Yet history rarely concerns itself with immediate outcomes.
Across Aedryn, stories of Karnhold spread faster than soldiers ever could. Whether entirely accurate or not, they carried a powerful message. The occupation could be resisted. More importantly, many believed it must be.
The rebellion had been extinguished. The idea behind it had not. For this reason, many historians identify the Night of Red Smoke not as the beginning of the Smoke War, but as the moment its arrival became inevitable.

Related Articles

lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-smok.filePath: Lore/History/The Smoke War/PC - The Smoke War.md
lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-smok.content:
At A Glance
The Smoke War was the defining conflict of modern Aedryn and the event most responsible for shaping the continent that exists today. Fought between the Dwarven Empire and the peoples of Aedryn, the war ultimately ended centuries of dwarven expansion and occupation across much of the continent.
The conflict began after decades of growing dwarven influence, fuelled by technological superiority, industrial might, and a belief that dwarven civilisation represented the future of Aedryn. What began as expansion gradually transformed into conquest, bringing large portions of the continent beneath dwarven control.
Following the Night of Red Smoke and the rise of widespread resistance movements, humans, elves, ratfolk, orcs and liberated automatons united beneath the banner of the Velmoran Alliance. Years of brutal warfare eventually led to the collapse of the empire and the liberation of occupied territories.
Although the war ended decades ago, its legacy remains visible throughout Aedryn. Political tensions, distrust of authority, economic hardship, cultural division and lingering prejudice can all be traced, in one way or another, to the scars left behind by the Smoke War.
Historical Account
Excerpt from The Fall of Empire: A Study of the Smoke War, authored by Professor Aldren Voss of the Astral Collegium, published 12 years after the conclusion of the conflict.

There are few events in modern history that have shaped Aedryn as profoundly as the Smoke War. Even now, decades after the final battles were fought, the conflict continues to influence politics, religion, trade, and the relationships between entire peoples.
To understand the war, one must first understand the world that existed before it.
Long before the modern era, the dwarves of the Stoneward Spine established themselves as the foremost industrial and military power upon the continent. While many nations pursued faith, magic, philosophy, or commerce, the dwarves devoted themselves almost entirely to engineering, production, and technological advancement. Their cities grew wealthy. Their armies became unmatched. Their influence spread steadily beyond the mountains.
What began as expansion eventually became conquest.
Many dwarven records from the period speak of a belief that their civilisation represented the natural future of Aedryn. Other cultures were often described as inefficient, unstable, or incapable of governing themselves properly. Whether this belief was born from arrogance, genuine conviction, or simple political necessity remains a matter of debate amongst scholars.
What is not debated is the result.
Within a generation, vast portions of the continent found themselves beneath dwarven occupation.
The event most commonly cited as the beginning of open rebellion is the tragedy now remembered as the Night of Red Smoke.
Unfortunately, reliable accounts remain scarce.
Velmoran records describe a peaceful protest that was violently suppressed by occupying forces. Surviving dwarven documents claim armed agitators provoked a military response. Independent accounts are frustratingly limited, often contradicting one another in significant ways.
Whatever the truth, the aftermath is far clearer.
Hundreds died.
Perhaps thousands.
The city of Karnhold became a symbol throughout the continent, and the names of those lost were carried far beyond its walls.
In the years that followed, resistance movements that had previously acted independently began to unite. Humans, elves, ratfolk, orcs, and eventually liberated automatons found common cause against a shared enemy. This alliance would become known as the Velmoran Alliance.
The war that followed was brutal.
Ratfolk operatives sabotaged supply lines and infrastructure hidden deep within dwarven territory. Orc tribes launched coordinated uprisings against those who had enslaved them. Automatons turned upon their former masters, bringing with them invaluable knowledge of dwarven logistics and military production.
Entire settlements vanished from maps.
Ancient forests burned.
Mountain strongholds collapsed.
The war consumed nearly every corner of Aedryn.
As the conflict dragged on, the empire began to fracture. Supply lines stretched thin. Internal divisions grew. Rumours persist of political betrayals within the highest levels of dwarven leadership, though many relevant records were lost during the final years of the conflict.
Eventually the empire collapsed.
The mountains were seized.
Former dwarven territories were divided amongst the victorious powers.
Peace returned to Aedryn.
At least officially.
The scars left by the Smoke War remain visible even today.
Many of the continent’s governments were shaped by wartime fears. Distrust of authority remains widespread. Entire peoples continue to carry inherited grievances against one another. Even younger generations who never witnessed the conflict often speak of it as though it happened yesterday.
The war is over.
Most agree on that much.
Whether its consequences have truly ended is a question that Aedryn still struggles to answer.

Archivist’s Note: While Professor Voss is widely regarded as one of the foremost historians of the modern era, critics have frequently accused the Astral Collegium of favouring Velmoran sources when discussing the Smoke War. Readers are encouraged to consult additional texts where available.

lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-velm.filePath: Lore/History/The Smoke War/PC - The Velmoran Alliance.md
lore/history/the-smoke-war/pc---the-velm.content:
At A Glance
The Velmoran Alliance was the coalition formed to oppose the Dwarven Empire during the Smoke War. It united humans, elves, ratfolk, orcs, automatons and countless smaller groups beneath a common cause for the first time in Aedryn’s history.
Though tensions and disagreements existed between its members, the Alliance transformed scattered resistance into an organised military force capable of challenging dwarven rule. Many historians regard its formation as the true beginning of the Smoke War.
The war that followed lasted for many years and left deep scars across the continent. Despite the devastation, the Alliance ultimately emerged victorious, bringing an end to the Dwarven Empire’s control of Aedryn.

Historical Account
Excerpt from The Rise of the Alliance, preserved within the archives of Velor.

History often remembers victories as inevitable.
The formation of the Velmoran Alliance was anything but.
For generations, the peoples of Aedryn had lived alongside one another without truly standing together. Old grudges lingered. Borders divided communities. Trade disputes, cultural differences and historical rivalries ensured that cooperation rarely extended beyond necessity.
The Dwarven Empire understood this well.
For many years, resistance movements arose throughout the continent. Some fought openly. Others operated from the shadows. Most were defeated in isolation.
The Night of Red Smoke changed that.
News of the massacre at Karnhold spread across Aedryn with a speed that surprised even those who carried the stories. Whether through outrage, fear or simple exhaustion, many began to realise that resistance would continue to fail so long as each people fought alone.
Slowly, alliances began to form.
Human settlements pledged soldiers and supplies. Ellei communities opened communication with neighbouring groups they had long ignored. Ratfolk networks carried information between resistance cells. Orc tribes rose against former masters. Even automatons, once created to serve the empire, began turning against it.
What emerged was unlike anything the continent had seen before.
Not a kingdom.
Not an empire.
An alliance.
The early years were difficult. Resources were scarce. Trust was limited. Victory was far from certain. Yet despite these challenges, the coalition endured.
As the war dragged on, so too did the Alliance grow.
Every victory inspired another uprising. Every setback strengthened the resolve of those who remained. Entire regions joined the cause, until what had once been a collection of isolated rebellions became a continent-wide movement.
The cost was staggering.
Cities burned.
Forests were scarred.
Generations were lost to conflict.
Yet by the war’s end, the impossible had become reality.
The Dwarven Empire had fallen.
Many of the nations that exist today trace their origins to agreements first forged within the Alliance. While the coalition itself would eventually dissolve, its legacy remains visible throughout Aedryn.
For a brief moment in history, peoples who had every reason to remain divided chose a different path.
And together, they changed the world.

lore/pc---magic.content:
At A Glance
Magic occupies a complicated place within modern Aedryn. While it remains an undeniable part of everyday life, it is also feared by much of the population. To many ordinary citizens, magic is associated with instability, catastrophe and forces beyond mortal understanding.
Most people cannot meaningfully distinguish between the various forms of magic. Arcane practitioners, divine miracle workers, occult scholars and other wielders of supernatural power are often viewed with the same mixture of awe and suspicion. For many, the question is not where the power comes from, but whether it can be trusted.
Attitudes towards magic vary greatly throughout the continent. Some regions encourage its study and development, while others regard public displays of magic with deep unease. Regardless of where one travels, however, few subjects provoke stronger opinions than the use of supernatural power.

lore/pc---peoples-of-aedryn.content:
Humans
Humans are the most numerous people within Aedryn and can be found in every region of the continent. While particularly associated with Velmora, human settlements exist throughout the world and encompass an enormous range of cultures, beliefs and professions.
Their adaptability and ambition have allowed them to thrive in almost every environment, making them one of the most influential peoples of the modern age.
Elves
Elves are most commonly associated with the Verdant Expanse and the Ellei. Known for their long lives and deep connection to history, many elves view themselves as caretakers of traditions that predate modern civilisation.
While often perceived as reserved by outsiders, elven communities possess a strong sense of identity and responsibility towards the natural world.
Vanara
Vanara are Monkey Folk  native to the Verdant Expanse and form a significant portion of the Ellei. Curious, energetic and highly social, they are often more willing than their elven counterparts to engage with the wider world.
Despite their reputation for mischief, many are accomplished scholars, scouts and diplomats.
Ratfolk
The Ratfolk of Aedryn are renowned for their ingenuity, adaptability and thirst for knowledge. Once forced to survive within the shadows of the Dwarven Empire, they now form the backbone of the Redentan and are responsible for many of the technological advancements emerging from the Stoneward Spine.
Many view the world as a puzzle waiting to be understood.
Orcs
Orcs played a significant role in the fall of the Dwarven Empire and today form an important part of the Folkmoor Creed. Their history is marked by resilience, perseverance and a determination never to be controlled again.
Contrary to many old stereotypes, modern orc communities are as diverse as any other people of Aedryn.
Automatons
Created by the Dwarven Empire, automatons were originally built to serve as labourers and soldiers. Their rebellion during the Smoke War became one of the defining moments of modern history.
Today, automatons can be found throughout Aedryn, pursuing lives of their own choosing and continuing to define what freedom means for a people who were once considered property.

lore/pc---the-rift.content:
At A Glance
The Rift is one of the greatest mysteries in modern Nerra. While its exact nature remains poorly understood, it is widely associated with magical instability, strange phenomena, and many of the unanswered questions surrounding the Cataclysm.
References to the Rift become increasingly common in historical records following the Cataclysm, leading many scholars to believe the two events are connected. Whether the Rift caused the disaster, emerged because of it, or merely became visible during the chaos remains a subject of fierce debate.
Throughout Aedryn, the Rift is often spoken of with a mixture of fear and fascination. Scholars seek to understand it. Religious institutions argue over its significance. Ordinary people tend to avoid it entirely. Few things inspire more speculation.
Account of a Survivor
Taken from the journal of an unnamed merchant recovered near the eastern trade roads, date unknown.

I first thought it was a storm.
The sky had gone wrong. Not dark. Wrong.
The clouds moved against the wind and the light seemed to bend around them. The horses noticed before any of us did. They refused to move forward.
Then we heard the sound.
It was like distant singing beneath water.
Not loud enough to understand. Just loud enough that you knew it was there.
One of the guards walked towards it. He said he could see lights between the trees.
We never saw him again.
We searched until dawn.
No tracks.
No blood.
No sign that he had ever existed beyond our memory.
We left before the sun rose fully.
I remember looking back once.
The trees were gone.
I swear upon every god that they were gone.
There was only mist.
I’ve crossed the Sunders. I’ve sailed through storms that should have sunk ships. I’ve stood in battle.
Nothing has ever frightened me like that place.
If that was truly the Rift, then I pray I never see it again.
